content.full_text:
90-349
jwu
FPPC

South Coast
AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT DISTRICT

9150 FLAIR DRIVE, EL MONTE, CA 91731 (818) 572-6200

May 15, 1990

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Opinion

Dear Ms. Donovan:

I am writing to request an opinion regarding the applicability of conflict of interest laws to certain contractors. I am counsel to the South Coast Air Quality Management District, a regional government agency with responsibility for regulating emissions of air contaminants from a variety of sources. The following circumstances lead me to request this opinion.

Under the Air Toxics Hot Spots Information and Assessment Act of 1987, Health and Safety Code Sections 44300-44384, the District is required to assess the risks to public health caused by emissions of toxic substances from facilities within its jurisdiction. To accomplish this assessment, facilities utilizing specified hazardous substances are required to determine and report their hazardous emissions to the District. Certain source facilities, which generally are industrial and commercial establishments, are initially required to submit an "inventory" plan describing the manner in which they propose to sample and analyze emissions to detect the presence of hazardous constituents. The District is required to approve, modify, or return a proposed inventory plan within 120 days of submission. Health & Saf. Code Sec. 44340. Within 180 days of approval of the plans, the source facilities are required to conduct sampling and analysis and submit the result to the District. Health & Saf. Code Sec. 44341. The emission data is to be used in determining the public health impacts of each facility's emissions. Health & Saf. Code Sec. 44360. The public is to be notified of these findings. The findings are to be used to more effectively implement emission control strategies. Health & Saf. Code Sec. 44301(g).

Due to the great number of sources subject to the above sampling and analysis requirements, and a lack of District staff resources, the agency has contracted with two private consulting firms, Arthur D. Little, Inc. (ADL) and Almega, Inc., to review inventory plans. The District received approximately 150 inventory plans requiring emissions sampling and analysis for review in fiscal year 1989-1990 and expects to receive up to 300 for review in fiscal year 1990-1991.

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
Page 2
May 15, 1990


A question of potential conflict of interest has arisen because it is possible
that ADL and Almega have contracted, or possibly will contract, to perform services
for source facilities that have submitted inventory plans which ADL or Almega
would review for the District. Such services could relate to a wide variety of matters
which may or may not affect air quality regulation. I am concerned that Almega and
ADL may qualify as "consultants" under FPPC regulations and be subject to conflict
of interest restrictions.

The facts relevant to determination of consultant status are as follows: Both
Almega and ADL review inventory plans without active District supervision. The
District provided the contractors with initial guidance regarding the required
manner of reviewing plans; however, subsequent District involvement is limited to
examining a small sample of the plans reviewed by ADL or Almega. While the
contractors do not have authority to officially approve, disapprove or modify
inventory plans, they do provide the District with recommendations regarding these
matters, and their recommendations are generally followed by the agency.

I am informed that both ADL and Almega are relatively large corporations.
For example, ADL has over 2,000 employees performing consulting services for
clients in 60 countries. Contracts range in size from modest general consulting
assignments to multi-million dollar programs. ADL revenues in 1989 were
approximately $290 million. Employees of ADL and Almega who review inventory
plans for the District may have also conducted work for the source facilities
submitting such plans. Such work may have been totally unrelated to air quality,
and may have been conducted for facilities other than those submitting inventory
plans.

The financial impacts which approval or disapproval of an inventory plan
could have on a source facility are not certain. At a minimum, a disapproval would
result in the need to prepare a new plan which could cost the source between five
and thousand dollars. Of potentially greater impact, approval of an inventory
plan which was inadequate to fully assess emissions of toxic or hazardous air
contaminants could result in a determination by this agency that the source poses
less of a public health threat. While the Air Toxics Hot Spots Act is merely an
inventory program at this time, it is expected that relatively large emitters will at
some time in the future be subject to regulatory restrictions on emissions. Such
restrictions could have significant financial impacts on a source.

The District is concerned that disqualification of ADL or Almega for a
significant number of industrial sources due to potential conflicts could hamper the
inventory program. ADL and Almega were the only respondents to the District's
request for proposals for contracts to review source test protocols.

I request an opinion regarding the applicability of state conflict of interest
laws to the above contractors. If it is concluded that ADL and Almega are
consultants subject to the Political Reform Act, please provide guidance regarding
required disclosures and disqualification. We are under a strict timetable for review

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
Page 3
May 15, 1990

of protocols and therefore would appreciate a response as soon as possible. I would
be happy to discuss this matter with you if you need further information. Please call
me at (818) 572-2045.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Peter M. Greenwald
Principal Deputy District Counsel

PMG:ie
(511FPPC)

I request an opinion regarding the applicability of state conflict of interest laws to the above contractors. If it is concluded that ADL and Almega are consultants subject to the Political Reform Act, please provide guidance regarding required disclosures and disqualification.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether private consulting firms (Arthur D. Little, Inc. and Almega, Inc.) hired to review inventory plans for the South Coast Air Quality Management District constitute "consultants" subject to the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest restrictions, given that these firms may simultaneously perform unrelated services for the same source facilities whose plans they are reviewing.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request document, not an FPPC response. The document contains a detailed question regarding consultant status and conflict of interest rules. The concluding section requests guidance but does not present an FPPC conclusion. OCR quality is generally good with minor scanning artifacts.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: A question of potential conflict of interest has arisen because it is possible that ADL and Almega have contracted, or possibly will contract, to perform services for source facilities that have submitted inventory plans which ADL or Almega would review for the District. Such services could relate to a wide variety of matters which may or may not affect air quality regulation. I am concerned that Almega and ADL may qualify as "consultants" under FPPC regulations and be subject to conflict of interest restrictions. I request an opinion regarding the applicability of state conflict of interest laws to the above contractors. If it is concluded that ADL and Almega are consultants subject to the Political Reform Act, please provide guidance regarding required disclosures and disqualification.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from the South Coast Air Quality Management District's Principal Deputy District Counsel seeking an FPPC opinion on whether two consulting firms (ADL and Almega) hired to review air toxics inventory plans have potential conflicts of interest under California's Political Reform Act, particularly since these firms may also perform unrelated work for the same facilities whose plans they are evaluating.
